data.content: The build fails because analyzers + `TreatWarningsAsErrors` convert style warnings to errors. Let me get a timing baseline, then implement optimizations.

arguments.command: cd /tmp/sv-e1a7e1a7cd854124b5d37465f586965e && dotnet clean -v quiet --nologo 2>/dev/null; dotnet build-server shutdown 2>/dev/null; echo "--- Baseline (suppresssing style errors) ---" && time dotnet build --no-incremental -v quiet --nologo -p:TreatWarningsAsErrors=false 2>&1

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a full picture. The build fails because `TreatWarningsAsErrors` is set to `true` and `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` is also `true`, which turns code style suggestions into errors. The build takes ~28 seconds even for this tiny project.

Let me analyze the issues:

1. **`EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` + `TreatWarningsAsErrors`**: All code style analyzers run on every build, turning style suggestions into errors. This is extremely slow for inner-loop development. These should be separated or scoped.

2. **`GenerateDocumentationFile` always on**: Generates XML docs for every build including local dev, adding overhead.
   
3. **Redundant `ProjectReference`s**: Several projects are pulling in transitive dependencies directly — `BusinessLogic` unnecessarily references `Common` when it's already available through `DataAccess`, `WebApi` pulls in `DataAccess` and `Common` when only `BusinessLogic` is needed, and `Tests` references everything when it only directly uses `BusinessLogic` and `Common`. Since SDK-style projects enable transitive references by default, these extra references force unnecessary evaluation work.

4. **Solution has 6 platform configurations** (Debug and Release across Any CPU, x64, and x86) even though all projects target only Any CPU — the x86 and x64 configurations are just adding build overhead.

5. **`AnalysisLevel` set to `latest-recommended`** enables the full suite of analyzers, and combined with `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild`, that's creating significant build weight.

6. The `TargetFramework` appears in both `Directory.Build.props` and each individual `.csproj`, though it looks like there might be some duplication or inconsistency in how it's being set across the files.

Now I'm thinking through which optimizations would have the biggest impact without breaking anything. The most valuable changes would be making the heavy analysis and documentation generation conditional on CI or Release builds rather than running them during inner-loop development, removing the redundant project references, eliminating the duplicate target framework declarations, and stripping out the unused platform configurations from the solution.

Before I implement these changes, I should get a clean baseline by temporarily disabling the warnings-as-errors setting so I can measure the actual build time, then apply the optimizations and compare.

data.content: Now I have the full picture. Let me implement all the optimizations.

arguments.old_str:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <!-- Documentation generated for every build, even during local dev -->
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Analyzers always on, even for dev inner loop -->
  <PropertyGroup>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <AnalysisLevel>latest-recommended</AnalysisLevel>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CS1591;CA1000</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
arguments.new_str:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Heavy analysis only in CI / Release builds to keep dev inner loop fast -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Release' or '$(CI)' == 'true'">
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CS1591;CA1000</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <AnalysisLevel>latest-recommended</AnalysisLevel>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
Good. The baseline is ~17-18 seconds for a tiny 5-project solution with very few source files. That's quite slow. Now let me implement the optimizations.

Here's my analysis and plan:

**Issues found:**

1. **`EnforceCodeStyleInBuild=true` + `TreatWarningsAsErrors=true` in Directory.Build.props** — Runs all Roslyn code-style analyzers on every build and promotes them to errors. This is the single biggest performance hit. Should be scoped to CI only.

2. **`GenerateDocumentationFile=true` always on** — Generates XML docs every build, unnecessary for local dev.


3. **Redundant transitive ProjectReferences** — Several projects are explicitly referencing dependencies they don't directly need. `BusinessLogic` pulls in `Common` transitively through `DataAccess`, `WebApi` only needs `BusinessLogic` directly, and `Tests` can rely on transitive references from `BusinessLogic` and `Common`.

4. **Redundant `TargetFramework` declarations** — Each project specifies this when it's already defined globally in `Directory.Build.props`.

5. **Unnecessary solution platform configs** — Extra configurations that aren't being used.

Now I'm going to apply these fixes across the project files.

arguments.old_str:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <!-- Documentation generated for every build, even during local dev -->
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Analyzers always on, even for dev inner loop -->
  <PropertyGroup>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <AnalysisLevel>latest-recommended</AnalysisLevel>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CS1591;CA1000</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>

data.reasoningText:
The build still fails because `AnalysisLevel=latest-recommended` is still on unconditionally, and that's elevating some style rules to errors (IDE0160 etc.) even without `TreatWarningsAsErrors` and `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild`. Wait, looking at the errors, they say "error IDE0160" — these should only be errors if `TreatWarningsAsErrors` is true. But I conditioned that on Release/CI...

Let me check — maybe `AnalysisLevel=latest-recommended` by itself sets some rules as errors? Or maybe there's an `.editorconfig` or something.


Actually, I think `AnalysisLevel=latest-recommended` might be configuring certain analyzer rules as errors by default, separate from `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild`. Since `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` defaults to false, the IDE code style rules shouldn't run during Debug builds at all — but these IDE0160, IDE0011, IDE0022 errors are definitely code style rules, so something's off with my understanding of when they should trigger.

Let me check if there's an `.editorconfig` file that might be overriding these settings.

data.reasoningText:
Interesting — the build is passing for Common.csproj with -v detailed. It looks like the IDE errors might only fire when we do the full solution build. Let me check if the properties are being picked up correctly. Also, I notice `GenerateDocumentationFile` is still generating `/doc:obj/Debug/net8.0/Common.xml` even though I made it conditional on Release/CI. Wait — `doc:` is only generated if `GenerateDocumentationFile` is true, and the NoWarn suppression includes CS1591... but the `NoWarn` is also conditional on Release/CI now. Let me check the properties more carefully.
 
Hmm, so the individual project builds fine but the solution build is failing with IDE analyzer errors showing as hard errors. I think `AnalysisLevel` is set to `latest-recommended` unconditionally in Directory.Build.props, which means some analyzers default to error severity. The `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` is only conditioned on Release/CI, so that might not be suppressing these during the solution build.

Let me trace through this more carefully — the individual `Common.csproj` build succeeded with exit code 0, so those IDE rules didn't trigger as errors there. But the solution-level build is hitting them. I should do a clean rebuild of the solution to rule out any caching issues.

data.reasoningText:
Wait, there's something odd here. The individual project build succeeds but the solution-level build fails. The errors are IDE code style rules (IDE0160, IDE0011, etc.) showing as "error" severity. Even though I've conditioned `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` and `TreatWarningsAsErrors` on Release/CI, they're still failing.

This might be caused by the `AnalysisLevel=latest-recommended` setting which I left unconditional. With `latest-recommended`, some analyzers may be configured with error severity. Or, perhaps there's something about how `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` defaults work in .NET 11 preview SDK.
 
The `AnalysisLevel=latest-recommended` could be setting IDE rules to error severity regardless of my conditions. But there's another possibility—in the .NET 11 preview, `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` might default to true, which would cause IDE rules to run during build even when I haven't explicitly enabled them.

I notice the individual project built successfully but the solution build failed, so there might be a difference in how solution-level and project-level builds handle these settings. Let me run a diagnostic build on the solution to see what `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` actually evaluates to.

data.content: .NET 11 SDK defaults `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` to `true`. I need to explicitly disable it for Debug builds.

arguments.old_str:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Heavy analysis only in CI / Release builds to keep dev inner loop fast -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Release' or '$(CI)' == 'true'">
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CS1591;CA1000</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <AnalysisLevel>latest-recommended</AnalysisLevel>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
arguments.new_str:
<Project>
  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <AnalysisLevel>latest-recommended</AnalysisLevel>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Keep dev inner loop fast: disable heavy analysis in Debug -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Debug' and '$(CI)' != 'true'">
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>false</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>false</GenerateDocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- Full analysis in Release / CI -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(Configuration)' == 'Release' or '$(CI)' == 'true'">
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <GenerateDocumentationFile>true</GenerateDocumentationFile>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <NoWarn>$(NoWarn);CS1591;CA1000</NoWarn>
  </PropertyGroup>
</Project>
